results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The call targets Troop/Police Contributing Countries (T/PCCs) and Participating UN Organizations (PUNOs) in the context of UN peacekeeping; while PUNOs are mentioned as budget recipients, the call is specifically for increasing women's participation in peace operations — not within the ILO's mandate areas of labour standards, employment, social protection, or decent work. Additionally, the Letter of Interest stage is closed and the page is now in a project development/template-sharing phase, not an open call with an active application window. Funding objective is also not ILO-implementable as it focuses on military/police uniformed women in peacekeeping, outside ILO programmatic scope.
results.classification_summary: The Elsie Initiative Fund Fourth Programming Round targets Troop and Police Contributing Countries (T/PCCs) and UN Participating Organizations to increase women's participation in uniformed peacekeeping roles. The Letter of Interest stage is explicitly closed, making this no longer an open call with an active application window. The fund's primary objective — gender parity in military/police peacekeeping deployments — falls outside ILO's mandate on labour standards, decent work, and social protection. As a result, the call fails on both the 'Is open call with active application window' and 'Funding objective is ILO-implementable' criteria.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: Eligible applicants are T/PCCs (government military/police entities) and Participating UN Organizations (PUNOs). While PUNOs include UN agencies, the ILO's mandate does not cover uniformed peacekeeping operations, and the call is framed exclusively for defence/police ministries and UN peacekeeping-related entities.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The Elsie Initiative Fund provides grant funding (non-reimbursable) through Flexible Project Funding and MOWIP barrier assessments, as evidenced by the budget templates and project documentation guidance.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: No specific maximum or minimum grant amounts are stated on the page or in the attached documents.

Deadline is future.evidence: The page explicitly states 'The call for Letters of Interest is now closed.' The LOI was the first mandatory submission step, and it has already passed.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The page explicitly states the Letter of Interest stage is closed and the page now serves only as a template repository for invited applicants. There is no active open application window.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The fund's primary purpose is to increase the participation and effectiveness of uniformed women (military and police) in UN peace operations. This is a peacekeeping/security-sector objective outside ILO's mandate on labour standards, employment, social protection, and decent work.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a grant programming round, not a procurement or tender process.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No explicit co-financing requirement is mentioned in the available page content or attached documents.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is required based on the available content.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium leadership rules are specified in the available content.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: Applicants are restricted to Troop/Police Contributing Countries (T/PCCs) — i.e., countries that contribute uniformed personnel to UN peace operations — and Participating UN Organizations, which effectively restricts eligibility by entity type and role.

results.title: Elsie Initiative Fund – Fourth Programming Round
results.deadline_text: The call for Letters of Interest is now closed.
theme: Decent Work in Crisis and Post-Crisis Situations

application_process.description: Letter of Interest stage – now closed
Full project proposal (PRODOC) development – for invited applicants only

eligible_activities: Training activities (conference room hire, training materials, IEC materials)
Female-fit equipment (body armour, helmets)
MOWIP barrier assessments including civilian research team travel
Recruitment of consultants or CSOs to deliver training
Accommodation for military/police members required to travel
Purchase of accounting software to manage the EIF grant
Contractual services with NGOs, CSOs, or other non-government entities as implementing partners
funding_restrictions: Purchase of any vehicle
Purchase of ammunition, weapons, riot shields or batons
Payment of salaries and overtime for government ministry employees, military/police, or uniformed personnel
Alcohol
Gifts for senior personnel or conference bags with gifts/mementoes/electronic equipment
results.proposal_summary: The Elsie Initiative Fund (EIF), supported by Global Affairs Canada, launched its Fourth Programming Round on 9 October 2025 to support Troop and Police Contributing Countries (T/PCCs) and Participating UN Organizations (PUNOs) in increasing the participation and effectiveness of uniformed women in UN peace operations. The fund provides non-reimbursable grants in USD through two modalities: Flexible Project Funding (FPF) and MOWIP Barrier Assessments. The Letter of Interest stage is now closed; the page currently serves as a resource hub for invited applicants developing full project proposals (PRODOCs). Grant amounts are not publicly specified. The call is not open to general applicants including the ILO, as it targets government defence/police ministries of T/PCCs and UN peacekeeping-related entities.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call focuses on gender parity in uniformed military and police peacekeeping deployments, which is a security-sector objective tangentially related to gender equality but outside ILO's core mandate on labour standards, decent work, employment, and social protection.

results.deadline_text: Submit your application by 1 July 2026, 23:00 CET

application_process.description: Info Session for potential applicants (online, 13:00 Brussels time)
Deadline for submission of online application (23:00 CET)
Selected applicants informed by email (up to 10 CSOs)
Stage 1 kick-off event in Chisinau (2-day offline event, 8-9 September 2026)
Final event in Chisinau and announcement of up to 3 campaign winners (mid-November 2026)
Stage 2: Campaign production and implementation starts (January 2027)
Campaign implementation ends (June 2027), followed by monitoring and evaluation

eligible_activities: Capacity building in strategic communication, audience analysis, messaging, storytelling, budgeting, media engagement, campaign planning, and evaluation
Individual mentorship for campaign development
Networking and peer learning activities
Campaign production: design, production, printing
Social media advertising
Public events and community engagement activities
Media work and explanatory materials
Fundraising drives and resource mobilisation campaigns
Campaigns promoting the 2% Designation Mechanism, individual donations, crowdfunding, membership schemes, local business partnerships, or non-financial support

Lead applicants under 18 years old
More than one application per organisation
Applications submitted after the deadline
results.proposal_summary: The EU-funded Eastern Partnership Civil Society Facility project, implemented by a GDSI-led consortium, invites officially registered civil society organisations (CSOs) from the Republic of Moldova to apply for the 2026 Communication Labs — a practical capacity-building programme to help CSOs develop and implement their own communication campaigns. Up to 10 CSOs will be selected to participate in training, mentoring, and networking activities (September–November 2026), with up to 3 selected campaigns receiving EU financial support of up to EUR 8,000 each for production and implementation (January–June 2027). Eligibility is restricted to CSOs officially registered and operating in Moldova with at least two years of experience. Applications must be submitted in English via the online form by 1 July 2026 at 23:00 CET. No co-financing is required, and participation in the programme is free of charge.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This call targets Moldovan CSOs for communication campaign capacity-building and does not align with the ILO's mandate on labour standards, employment, social protection, social dialogue, or decent work.

results.proposal_summary: The European External Action Service (EEAS) is calling for session proposals for the EU Community of Practice on Peace Mediation 2026 (CoP2026), to be held in Brussels on 20-21 October 2026, under the theme 'Peace Mediation in the Age of Power Politics.' This is not a financial grant but an open call for civil society organisations, mediation practitioners, and government/multilateral representatives to design and convene sessions covering priority areas such as ceasefire mediation, protection of track 1 mediators, non-state armed actor engagement, maritime security, and inter-state conflict implications. No funding is awarded; session conveners are expected to cover their own speaker travel costs, with only exceptional EU support possible. The deadline for submissions is 15 June 2026, and proposals should be sent by email to COP-EACEMEDIATION@eeas.europa.eu. The call is globally open with a preference for participation from the Global South.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call focuses on peace mediation and conflict resolution, which is tangentially related to the ILO's work in crisis and post-crisis situations but does not align with the ILO's core mandate on labour standards, employment, social protection, or decent work.

results.proposal_summary: The European Union (through the GOJUST programme administered by UNOPS) has launched Window 3 of its Access to Justice Grant Facility in the Philippines, supporting initiatives that strengthen coordination and collaboration between academia, civil society, and community-based organizations to make the justice system work for women, Indigenous Peoples, and those in vulnerable situations. Grants of up to USD 250,000 are available to consortia of three members operating within or near designated Justice Zones. The call targets Philippine-based civil society, academic, and community organizations; UN agencies and intergovernmental organizations are not listed as eligible applicants. The first mandatory step — submission of an Expression of Interest — had a deadline of May 29, 2026 (now expired), with final proposals due August 31, 2026. Inquiries can be directed to gojustgrants@unops.org.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the call addresses access to justice for vulnerable groups including women and Indigenous Peoples — areas tangentially related to ILO's mandate on fundamental rights — it is primarily a rule-of-law/justice-sector initiative rather than a labour standards, employment, social protection, or decent work programme, and ILO is not an eligible applicant.

results.proposal_summary: The European External Action Service (EEAS) is calling for voluntary session proposals for the 2026 EU Community of Practice on Peace Mediation (CoP), its flagship annual event bringing together government representatives, policymakers, mediation practitioners, experts, and civil society from around the world. The event will take place in Brussels on 20-21 October 2026. This is not a grant call — no financial funding is offered. Session conveners are invited to submit proposals for panels, workshops, or interactive dialogues by 15 June 2026 using the provided template, with the aim of sharing lessons learned and best practices on peace mediation. No co-financing, accreditation, or geographic restrictions apply.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call concerns peace mediation and conflict resolution, which is tangentially related to ILO's work in crisis contexts (decent work in crisis/post-crisis situations), but has no direct connection to ILO's core mandate of labour standards, employment, social protection, or social dialogue, and offers no financial grant.

The eligible applicant category is explicitly framed around MSMEs and business support actors, with no mention of UN agencies, IGOs, or international organisations as eligible applicants. Additionally, the extended submission deadline of 30 April 2026 has passed as of today's date. The call's primary purpose is private-sector/MSME development, which is outside ILO's mandate as an implementing partner.

No mention of UN agencies, IGOs, or international organisations as eligible applicant types.

results.proposal_summary: The European Union, through the EU-Philippine Green Economy Partnership (60 million EUR total), is funding the 'Green Up to Scale Up: MSME Transformation Grant' managed by Expertise France and co-implemented with the Department of Trade and Industry (DTI) and the Global Green Growth Institute (GGGI) Philippines. The grant provides 1.8 million euros (PHP 125 million) in financial assistance and technical support to networks of actors enabling Filipino MSMEs in agriculture/fisheries, tourism/hospitality, food, and textile sectors to transition to circular and green business practices. Eligible applicants are Philippines-based networks/organisations supporting MSMEs. The application deadline was extended to 30 April 2026, which has now passed. The call does not list UN agencies or IGOs among eligible applicants.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the call touches on sustainable enterprises and green economy themes adjacent to ILO's just transition mandate, its primary focus is on MSME private-sector circular economy transformation in the Philippines, with no component related to labour standards, employment policy, social protection, or decent work.

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Circular Economy Facility (ACEF) is a trust fund established by the African Development Bank in 2022, supported by Finland, the Nordic Development Fund, and the Coca-Cola Foundation, dedicated to mainstreaming circular economy as an inclusive and green growth strategy across Africa. ACEF provides grants and technical assistance under three pillars: enabling environment for circularity, circular business development, and advocacy and knowledge sharing. The facility is active across African Development Bank member countries in Africa. No specific open call for proposals, application deadline, grant size range, or eligible applicant categories are published on this page, which functions as a programme description/landing page rather than an active CfP.
results.ilo_match_evidence: ACEF focuses on circular economy and green growth in Africa, which intersects with ILO's just transitions mandate, but the primary objective is environmental/economic transformation rather than labour standards, decent work, or social protection.

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Climate Change Fund (ACCF) is a multi-donor trust fund managed by the African Development Bank, established in 2014 and supported by donors including Germany, Belgium (Flanders), Italy, Canada, Austria, Ireland, the United States, the Province of Quebec, and the Global Center on Adaptation. It provides small grants to African governments, NGOs, and regional institutions to support climate-resilient, low-carbon development across African countries, with a focus on NDC implementation, climate finance access, adaptation and mitigation actions, and gender equality. No specific open call, grant size range, submission deadline, or application mechanism is described on this page, which functions as a general programme landing page.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the ACCF supports climate resilience and gender equality themes adjacent to ILO's just transitions mandate, the ILO is not among eligible applicant types (restricted to African governments, NGOs, and regional institutions), and the page does not represent an open call.

results.title: Africa Climate Risk Insurance Framework for Adaptation (ACRIFA)
theme: Just Transitions towards Environmentally Sustainable Economies and Societies
eligible_activities: Deployment of concessional high-risk capital through debt, subordinated-debt, first-loss, equity, and guarantees for climate insurance solutions
Value chain insurance, credit insurance, parametric insurance, and cross-border risk sharing mechanisms
Technical assistance grants to insurance providers, regulators, and value chain actors
Training and upskilling of agri-climate insurance professionals
Product development, data and digital solutions, and monitoring systems for agri-climate insurance
results.proposal_summary: ACRIFA is an African Development Bank initiative aimed at deploying up to $1 billion in concessional high-risk capital and grants to develop and scale climate risk insurance solutions for Africa's agricultural sector. The facility operates through two windows: an investment facility using debt, equity, and guarantee instruments for primary insurers and reinsurers, and a technical assistance facility providing grants to insurance providers, regulators, and agri-climate service providers. It targets African countries across all sub-regions. The page is a programme description, not an active call for proposals, with no submission deadline or defined application process.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the programme touches on climate adaptation and agricultural resilience which have tangential links to decent work, its primary focus is on mobilising private capital through insurance and investment instruments, which falls outside the ILO's core mandate.

results.proposal_summary: The African Beneficial Ownership Transparency Network (AfBOTN) is an initiative of the African Development Bank, in partnership with the UK Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office (FCDO) and other partners, launched in 2023 to bring international partners and African governments together to advance beneficial ownership transparency reforms across Africa. The network aims to increase visibility of international assistance, facilitate peer learning, and create a forum for identifying shared implementation challenges. It targets African governments that have made or are making commitments to beneficial ownership transparency, as well as donor agencies providing technical assistance in this area. No grant amounts, application deadlines, or open call for proposals are associated with this page; it is a general programme description of an ongoing network initiative.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The initiative focuses on beneficial ownership transparency, anti-money laundering, and illicit financial flows reform, which is tangentially related to ILO's governance-adjacent work but does not align with core ILO mandate areas of labour standards, employment, social protection, or decent work.

results.title: Uganda Refugee Support, Empowerment and Transformation (ReSET) Project: Call for Proposals
results.organization: United Kingdom Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office
theme: Decent Work in Crisis and Post-Crisis Situations

application_process.description: Submission of 7-page concept note

eligible_activities: Meeting core humanitarian needs (food security, protection) of vulnerable refugees and host communities
Multisectoral assistance for new arrivals and acutely vulnerable populations
Self-reliance and resilience pathways for economically vulnerable refugee and host community households
Programmes targeting women, girls, youth, and persons with disabilities
Gender equality and social inclusion activities
Climate resilience programming
Social cohesion and localisation activities
Safeguarding Uganda's inclusive refugee policy
results.proposal_summary: The Uganda Refugee Support, Empowerment and Transformation (ReSET) Project is a bilateral programme funded by the UK Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO) via the British High Commission in Kampala. The programme supports Uganda's transition from a humanitarian-only refugee response to one emphasising self-reliance and resilience for refugees and host communities. The total fund is up to £20 million over 2.5 years (August 2026 to March 2029). Eligible applicants are NGOs (international, national, or refugee-led) registered under the Uganda NGO Bureau. Individual organisations and consortia may submit a 7-page concept note by 11:59pm East Africa Time on 16 June 2026, proposing activities under Pillar 1 (core humanitarian needs), Pillar 2 (self-reliance/resilience), or both. Co-financing is not required. The ILO is not eligible as the call is restricted to NGOs.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call addresses livelihoods, social protection, gender equality, and resilience for refugees and host communities in Uganda, which are areas relevant to ILO's decent work and employment mandate, though the primary focus is humanitarian assistance rather than core labour standards or employment policy work.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO is not eligible: the call restricts applicants to non-profit organisations (NGOs, research institutions, universities, foundations, UK-based non-profits, educational institutions) and does not mention UN agencies, intergovernmental organisations, or equivalent international bodies.
results.classification_summary: This FCDO call is restricted to legally constituted non-profit organisations including NGOs, research institutions, universities, foundations, and UK-based non-profits. The ILO, as a UN intergovernmental organisation, does not fall within any of these named eligible categories, and no equivalent international/UN entity type is listed. The call is otherwise a genuine, currently-open grant with a future deadline of 19 June 2026 and a funding value above £1,000,000, focused on Indonesia's energy transition.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: Eligible organisations are explicitly named as NGOs, UK-based non-profit organisations, and educational institutions (research institutions, universities, foundations). UN agencies, intergovernmental organisations, or IGOs are not mentioned. The list is exhaustive and does not include ILO-type entities.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The call explicitly uses 'accountable grant' and references an 'Accountable Grant Arrangement Template', confirming a non-reimbursable grant instrument.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The funding value is stated as 'More than £1,000,000', well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The application deadline is 11:59pm Western Indonesia Time (GMT+7) on 19 June 2026, which is after today's date of 25 May 2026.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The page describes a single specific call (MENTARI 2) with a concrete submission deadline of 19 June 2026, an application email address (mentari2@fcdo.gov.uk), and downloadable application templates. Published 22 May 2026.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The call focuses on renewable energy policy reform, investment support/bankability of small-scale renewable energy projects, and energy transition in Indonesia. While GEDSI mainstreaming is included, the primary objective is energy sector investment support and policy reform, not labour standards or decent work programming.

results.proposal_summary: The UK Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO) is inviting proposals under the MENTARI 2 programme, a bilateral UK-Indonesia partnership supporting Indonesia's energy transition and implementation of the UK-Indonesia Strategic Partnership in the energy sector. The call funds activities in the areas of energy policy reform, investment support for small-scale renewable energy projects, and mainstreaming of gender equality, disability, and social inclusion (GEDSI). The grant is valued at more than £1,000,000 and will support implementation from July 2026 to March 2028. Eligible applicants are legally constituted non-profit organisations including NGOs, research institutions, universities, foundations, and UK-based non-profits; UN agencies and intergovernmental organisations are not eligible. The application deadline is 19 June 2026, and proposals must be submitted via email to mentari2@fcdo.gov.uk. No co-financing requirement is stated.

Additionally, the EOI deadline of 3 June 2026 has passed relative to today's date of 2026-06-08.

results.proposal_summary: The UK Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO) launched a £30 million, 2.5-year (2026–2029) accountable grant programme — Humanitarian Emergency and Longer-term Planning Sudan (HELP-S) — aimed at strengthening local responders and community resilience in more-stable parts of conflict-affected Sudan. Eligible applicants include international NGOs, Sudanese civil society organisations, United Nations agencies, not-for-profit academic institutions, and mixed consortia (with for-profit entities limited to downstream partner roles). Applicants were required to submit a 5-page Expression of Interest and high-level budget by email to Daniel.diriba@fcdo.gov.uk by 11:59pm EAT on 3 June 2026 (extended from 27 May 2026). No co-financing requirement is stated. The call is targeted exclusively at Sudan, and the ILO, as a UN agency, was formally eligible; however, the submission deadline has now passed.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the programme targets decent work in crisis settings and community resilience in Sudan — areas with ILO relevance — the call's primary focus is humanitarian emergency response and locally-led civil society delivery rather than the ILO's core mandate areas of labour standards, employment policy, social protection systems, or social dialogue.

results.proposal_summary: The UK Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO) is inviting proposals for the protection component of the Jordan Humanitarian and Systems Strengthening Programme (JHASS). The programme will provide up to £7 million through an accountable grant agreement to a single NGO-led consortium to deliver protection services — including GBV prevention and response, child protection, psychosocial support, and community-based protection — for refugees and vulnerable Jordanians in non-camp settings. The programme is expected to run from September 2026 to March 2030, subject to annual funding confirmation. Eligible lead organisations must be non-profit INGOs legally registered in Jordan and their country of incorporation, with relevant humanitarian protection experience; national and local NGOs/CBOs are strongly encouraged as consortium partners. Commercial and for-profit organisations are not eligible as lead organisations. Full proposals must be submitted by 28 June 2026, with clarification questions due by 1 June 2026.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call addresses child labour, GBV, social inclusion, and protection of vulnerable workers/refugees, which partially overlaps with ILO themes on fundamental rights at work and decent work in crisis settings, but the primary focus is humanitarian protection programming led by INGOs rather than labour standards or employment policy work.

results.proposal_summary: The Brazil-UK PACT programme, funded by the UK Government through FCDO and DESNZ via UK International Climate Finance, launched a Call for Proposals in October 2025 seeking one initiative to strengthen federative climate governance and enhance implementation of Brazil's Climate Plan in Brazil. The call prioritised proposals that build capacity of public managers, foster cross-government collaboration at sub-national levels, align national NDC goals with sub-national instruments, and establish measurable indicators. No grant amount is specified in the public notice. The application deadline was 15 December 2025 and this call is now closed.

results.proposal_summary: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI) – China (2026) is a Global Affairs Canada programme that funds small-scale, high-impact local development projects in China. Grants range from approximately CAD 30,000 to a maximum of CAD 100,000. Eligible recipients include local NGOs and community organisations, local academic institutions, international NGOs, intergovernmental and multilateral organisations (including UN agencies) working on local development activities, and Canadian non-profit organisations. Projects must align with at least one of two thematic priorities: democratic governance and human rights (including gender equality), or inclusive growth/women's economic empowerment/green development. results.proposal_summary: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI) – Ukraine 2026 is an annual small-grant program administered by the Embassy of Canada to Ukraine under Global Affairs Canada. It supports small-scale, high-impact projects aligned with Canada's thematic development priorities, conceived and designed predominantly by local actors in Ukraine. Grants average CAD 30,000–50,000 with a maximum of CAD 100,000, disbursed as non-reimbursable contributions converted to Ukrainian hryvnia. Projects must be completed between the Contribution Agreement signature (approximately August/September 2026) and March 15, 2027. Eligible applicants are local organizations and civil society actors in Ukraine; UN agencies and intergovernmental organizations are not among the named eligible categories. The deadline for preliminary proposal submission is June 10, 2026 at 23:59 GMT+3, with applicants notified of status by June 29, 2026.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the CFLI may support activities touching on social issues in crisis-affected Ukraine, the program is explicitly designed for small-scale local civil society projects and does not align with the ILO's mandate as an intergovernmental technical assistance organization implementing labour standards, employment, and social protection programmes.

results.proposal_summary: Norad (Norwegian Agency for Development Cooperation), funded by the Norwegian government with an ambition of NOK 1 billion for 2025–2028, is seeking full project applications under Stage 2 (by invitation only) of its development programme to reduce plastic pollution and marine litter in developing countries. Priority countries are Indonesia, Vietnam, Kenya, Ghana, and Tanzania, with global and regional projects also accepted. The call is open exclusively to invited non-governmental organisations as lead applicants; for-profit organisations, foreign governmental institutions, multilateral organisations, and intergovernmental organisations may participate only as consortium members. The call invites studies that identify, develop, or test strategies to overcome barriers to adoption or scale-up of evidence-based health interventions, as well as de-implementation research on harmful or ineffective practices. results.proposal_summary: Global Affairs Canada (GAC) issued a call for concept notes to fund projects supporting the delivery of basic needs services—health, education, and women's and girls' rights and empowerment—to Afghans, with a focus on women and girls in Afghanistan. The total funding envelope was up to CAD$35,000,000 over four years, with individual grants anticipated at CAD$2,000,000 to CAD$10,000,000 each (4–8 projects). Eligible applicants were required to register through the Partners@International portal and demonstrate a mandatory cost-sharing contribution of at least 5% of total eligible direct project costs. The call used a two-stage application process, with the first-stage concept note deadline on April 16, 2025. The funding opportunity is now closed.

results.organization: Sweden (Sida – Swedish International Development Cooperation Agency)

application_process.description: Step 1: Submission of concept notes (deadline 16 August 2026 at 17:00 CEST)

eligible_activities: Strengthening research capacity in health and/or SRHR in LMICs or Ukraine

Equitable research cooperation partnerships including co-production of proposals

Gender equality integration in research and development activities
Research activities embedding knowledge production in local contexts
funding_restrictions: No support solely or primarily for individual Masters or PhD degrees
No support solely or primarily for networking activities or conferences
No support solely or primarily for design or delivery of Masters or PhD programmes or courses
Concept notes must not exceed five pages (11pt font, no additional annexes)
results.proposal_summary: Sida (Sweden) invites concept notes for health/SRHR development research cooperation in low and lower-middle income countries (LMICs) or Ukraine. The call seeks to support research cooperation partnerships between Swedish organisations and organisations in LMICs or Ukraine, contributing to research, strengthened research capacity, and research impact in human health and/or sexual and reproductive health and rights. Eligible applicants are Swedish universities/research organisations with strong links to LMICs or Ukraine, or international universities/research organisations partnering with at least one Swedish research organisation. Grant amounts range from SEK 8–14 million per initiative, with implementation periods of 18–24 months feasible between January 2027 and December 2028. The call opened on 25 May 2026 with a concept note deadline of 16 August 2026; selected applicants will be invited to submit full proposals by 31 October 2026. Co-financing is welcomed but not required.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This call focuses on health and SRHR research cooperation, which falls outside the ILO's core mandate areas of labour standards, employment, social protection, and decent work, and the eligible applicant categories (universities and research organisations) explicitly exclude UN agencies such as the ILO.

results.proposal_summary: This page is an informational update from Sida (Sweden's development cooperation agency) addressed to organisations that applied under the CSO Strategy 2025–2029. The first call has already been processed, with indicative amounts communicated to applicants in June 2025. A new call for proposals for major grants is planned for 2027, with new agreements from 2028. Eligibility is restricted to civil society organisations; UN agencies and intergovernmental organisations such as the ILO are not eligible. No funding amounts or submission deadline are stated on this page.

results.title: Cabo Verde Blu-X Facility 2.0
theme: Just Transitions towards Environmentally Sustainable Economies and Societies
eligible_activities: Blue economy development (ocean innovation, marine conservation finance)
Sustainable tourism development (eco-experiences)
Ocean technology development
Climate-resilient livelihood creation for coastal communities
Marine conservation finance
results.proposal_summary: The Cabo Verde Blu-X Facility 2.0 is a $50M blended-finance investment vehicle managed under the Joint SDG Fund's invest platform, aimed at catalysing over $200M in public and private investment to transform Cabo Verde into Africa's leading blue economy hub. The facility offers a 4%+ coupon yield on blue bonds and targets institutional ESG investors, DFIs, impact investors, sovereign wealth funds, and diaspora networks over a 3–5 year investment period. It is focused on ocean innovation, sustainable tourism, marine conservation, and coastal community livelihoods in Cabo Verde, with an ambition to serve as a replicable model for other Small Island Development States. The page presents an investor pitch rather than an open Call for Proposals — there is no submission deadline, no grant instrument, and the primary mechanism is private capital mobilisation through blue bonds.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the facility mentions creating livelihoods for coastal communities, its primary purpose is private capital mobilisation through blue bonds — far outside the ILO's mandate of technical assistance on labour standards, decent work, and social protection.

results.title: Rwanda Cross-Border Digital Trade Platform

application_process.description: Contact investment team via email to express interest

results.proposal_summary: The Joint SDG Fund is promoting the Rwanda Cross-Border Digital Trade Platform, an investment mobilisation vehicle targeting $50M in capital (catalysing $200M+ in additional public and private investment) to scale Rwanda's digital cross-border trade infrastructure across border corridors with DRC, Uganda, Tanzania, and Burundi. The platform aims to provide trade financing for 25,000+ traders and SMEs (70% women-led), digitalize trade processes, and create sustainable livelihoods for 100,000+ border community members. The fund offers financial returns of 4-6% on trade finance guarantees and 15-20% IRR, with a 4-5 year funding horizon. Eligible 'applicants' are investors such as DFIs, impact investors, and commercial banks — not grant recipients. No grant instrument, submission deadline, or formal call for proposals exists; the page is an investment pitch on the Joint SDG Fund's investment portal.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the platform targets women-led SMEs and cross-border traders — populations relevant to ILO's decent work mandate — the vehicle is a private-capital investment fund with IRR targets, not a programmatic grant aligned with ILO's technical assistance and labour standards work.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO is not among the eligible applicant types listed in the NOFO. While 'Public International Organizations' is listed, the ILO's mandate (labour standards, employment, social protection) does not align with this call's primary purpose of documenting and reporting religious freedom abuses in Nigeria. More decisively, the funding objective — accountability for religious freedom abuses — is outside the ILO's mandate and not ILO-implementable.

The funding objective is not ILO-implementable, making the ILO ineligible for this opportunity.

results.proposal_summary: The U.S. Department of State's Office of International Religious Freedom (IRF) is offering a grant or cooperative agreement of up to USD 3,500,000 to improve documentation, reporting, and accountability for religious freedom abuses in Nigeria. The program targets activities in Nigeria, particularly the Middle Belt region, over a 24–48 month implementation period starting approximately 4 months after award. Eligible applicants include foreign-based and U.S.-based NGOs, Public International Organizations, institutions of higher education, and for-profit organizations; consortia are permitted with a single prime applicant. The call focuses on building civil society capacity to safely document abuses by state and non-state actors (including Boko Haram, ISIS-WA, and Fulani Ethnic Militias), engage accountability mechanisms, and pursue legal remedies. No cost-sharing is required. The application deadline is July 9, 2026.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This call focuses exclusively on documenting and reporting religious freedom abuses in Nigeria for accountability purposes, with no connection to the ILO's mandate areas of labour standards, employment policy, social protection, or decent work.

results.proposal_summary: The AdaptAction programme, co-financed by the European Union and AFD (Agence Française de Développement), is launching a call for action-research projects on climate change adaptation in North Africa and the Middle East, covering Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, Egypt, Lebanon, Palestine, Jordan, and Syria. The call aims to fund up to three research projects with a total budget of €310,000 and a maximum of €160,000 per project; projects must begin in Q4 2026 and conclude by 30 April 2028 (maximum 16 months). Eligible applicants are consortia led by a national university or research centre from at least one of the eight programme countries, combined with an internationally referenced institution or researcher; team members may include NGOs, think tanks, consultancies, and individual consultants. The first mandatory stage is the Expression of Interest (AMI), which closes on 3 July 2026; pre-selected applicants will be invited to submit full proposals by 25 September 2026. No co-financing is required and no UN or IGO entity type is listed among eligible applicants.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This call is focused on climate change adaptation research (water, agriculture, coastal zones) in the Mediterranean region and does not address labour standards, employment, social protection, decent work, or any other ILO core mandate area.

Reconstruction projects in Ukraine implemented in collaboration with the Swiss private sector

results.proposal_summary: SECO (Switzerland's State Secretariat for Economic Affairs) has launched the third call for proposals under its Ukraine country programme 2025–2028, providing up to CHF 50 million in co-financing for Swiss private-sector investment in Ukraine's reconstruction. The call is open from 27 May to 20 September 2026. Eligible applicants are Swiss companies with an established presence in Ukraine; individual grants range from CHF 3 million to CHF 10 million, with companies required to contribute at least 50% of project costs. Supported activities focus on investment in long-term production facilities in Ukraine. Projects are selected via a two-stage process by a joint Swiss-Ukrainian committee. The ILO is not an eligible applicant as the call is exclusively targeting Swiss private-sector companies.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call focuses exclusively on private-sector investment in physical production facilities in Ukraine and is restricted to Swiss companies, with no alignment to ILO's mandate on labour standards, employment policy, social protection, or decent work.
